topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone screen, jarringly bright against the muted backdrop of my study notes. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My heart gave a sudden, uncomfortable lurch. I froze, my pen hovering over the textbook, the silence of the library suddenly amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I slowly tapped the notification open. There, a recent photo of me, taken seemingly just hours ago at the school event, with a caption that was innocuous enough—a comment about the class discussion. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I quickly minimized the app, my fingers trembling slightly as I tried to steady my breathing. I glanced up, scanning the empty rows of desks, half-expecting to see him lurking in the periphery. The air in the room felt suddenly thin, charged with an unwelcome awareness.

[story 2]

I kept checking my phone, a compulsive habit that felt increasingly desperate. Every time I looked, the screen offered another glimpse into Mr. Harrison’s digital footprint. A story update, a tagged post, a seemingly casual interaction—each piece of information chipped away at my composure. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I started deliberately moving, shifting my weight from one foot to the other, trying to appear engrossed in my assignment, but my focus kept fracturing. When he posted a picture of a coffee shop, the casualness of it felt like a deliberate probe. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a prickling sensation across my skin that made me want to retreat entirely. I forced myself to concentrate on the quadratic equation in front of me, praying the feeling would subside.

[story 3]

It happened during the break. I was retrieving a water bottle, and my phone buzzed. It was a direct message from the instructor. My breath hitched. I walked quickly toward the water fountain, trying to maintain a normal stride, but my steps felt jerky, too fast. I pulled out my phone under the guise of checking the time, and there it was: a recent photo of me from a group project, clearly taken from a distance, and a message referencing something specific we had discussed in class. A wave of intense unease washed over me, leaving a clammy feeling on my palms. I swallowed hard, my throat suddenly dry. I looked around the crowded hallway, scanning faces, searching for any sign of him, any indication that this digital intrusion was part of a larger pattern. The noise of the hallway seemed overwhelming, yet I felt intensely isolated.
